one_line_takeaway: By decomposing the segmentation attack gradient into diffusion-model conditional/unconditional scores, the method generates transferable black-box adversarial attacks on segmentation without training any task-specific victim or surrogate segmentation model, at the cost of weaker performance on multi-class semantic segmentation than on binary segmentation tasks.
key_finding: Because the method needs no segmentation-specific surrogate, its perturbations are identical regardless of which surrogate backbone is nominally used (the 'Ours' row is the same across Table I, using ViT-derived setup, and Table II, using ResNet50-derived setup) -- unlike baselines, which perform best only on their matching surrogate/victim pair (e.g., MI-FGSM attacking a ViT victim from a ViT surrogate reaches M=.264, an effectively white-box-favorable case) but degrade off-diagonal. The paper's method is not uniformly best in-distribution but wins on cross-architecture transfer and query efficiency: on Vgg as victim (Table I/II), Ours reaches M=.160/CC=.270 versus best baseline MI-FGSM's M=.134/CC=.368. On the ensemble-adversarially-trained robust ResNet50 (Table VIII), Ours achieves M=.074/CC=.483 versus the best baseline RAP's M=.056/CC=.574. With only 100 queries (Table IV, 'Ours query', labeled 0.1k), the method's MAE often exceeds or matches query-based baselines using far more queries (e.g., 1k-17k), such as beating IASA (1k queries) on ResNet50 victim in MAE (.145 vs .089) though trailing it on CC (.323 vs .227) -- a mixed, metric-dependent result the authors summarize as 'on par with the query-based black-box attack methods with 1000 queries.' On semantic segmentation (Table III), the method also produces transferable attacks (e.g., mIoU drops from baseline 0.716 to 0.520 on PSPR50 victim) but the authors state it is generally weaker relative to baselines here than on COD.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a victim-model-agnostic method for generating black-box adversarial attacks on segmentation models. Its central derivation (Eq. 4-8) is that the conditional segmentation score needed to craft an attack, s_theta(y|x_adv), can be decomposed via Bayes' rule as s_theta(x_adv|y) - s_theta(x_adv) -- the conditional and unconditional image-generation scores of a diffusion model -- eliminating the need for any task-specific (segmentation) victim or surrogate model to compute the attack gradient. A single UNet-based conditional diffusion model is trained with a stochastic conditioning variable (Eq. 14) so it can estimate both the conditional and unconditional scores from one network, and these scores drive an iterative perturbation-accumulation procedure (Eqs. 10-13, Algorithm 1) that optionally incorporates victim-model queries to pick the best perturbation across iterations.
threat_model: Black-box adversarial attack against segmentation models (binary: camouflaged object detection/COD; multi-class: semantic segmentation; and an added extension to salient object detection). The attacker has no access to the victim model's architecture, weights, or gradients, and -- distinct from standard transfer-based black-box attacks -- does not train or rely on any task-specific segmentation surrogate model either; it only needs a generic diffusion-based conditional/unconditional image-generation model trained on a disjoint dataset. Victim-model queries are optional: the method can run purely query-free, or use a limited query budget (e.g., 100 queries, i.e. 0.1k) to select the best perturbation from a fixed iteration budget. Perturbations are l_infinity bounded with max magnitude delta_d = 8/255 and step size mu = 2/255.
limitations: The paper explicitly names two limitations. (1) In multi-class semantic segmentation the method cannot as easily target the most vulnerable misclassification direction as gradient-based approaches that directly use surrogate-model loss, because its perturbation is derived from diffusion scores rather than classification errors directly -- termed the 'multi-peak problem' by the authors. (2) There is only an implicit/weak correlation between classification error and the diffusion score estimate, which the authors say causes reduced effectiveness in some scenarios and flag as future work ('a deep correlation exploration between classification error and score will be investigated further').

datasets_used: COD10K (4,040 training images for victim models; test set for evaluation)
NC4K (4,121 images, used to train the condition-based score estimation/surrogate model)
PASCAL VOC 2012 (10,582 training images split evenly between victim and surrogate; validation set used for evaluation)
DUTS (10,553 training images for SOD victim models; test set for evaluation)
Aggregated SOD/DUT/ECSSD/PASCAL-S/SOC test sets (8,128 images total, used to train the SOD score-estimation model)
quantitative_results: Table I/II (COD, Vgg victim): Ours M=.160, CC=.270 vs best transfer baseline MI-FGSM M=.134, CC=.368
Table VIII (robust ResNet50 victim, PVTv2 surrogate): Ours M=.074, CC=.483 vs best baseline RAP M=.056, CC=.574
Table IV (COD, query-based, ResNet50 victim): Ours with 100 queries (0.1k) achieves M=.145, CC=.323 vs IASA with 1,000 queries (1k) M=.089, CC=.227
Table III (semantic segmentation, PSPR50 victim/surrogate): baseline mIoU=0.716 drops to 0.520 (Ours) vs 0.408 for best baseline IIA
Table V ablation: performance stable across omega in {30,50,70,90,150,200} on COD (CC ranges .323-.339, Sα .567-.577)
Table VI ablation: full method (noise + clip) reaches M=.145, CC=.323 vs no-noise variant M=.093/.092, showing the diffusion-style noise injection step is necessary
Table VII ablation: m_max sweep from 20-70 shows M ranging .110-.123, CC .364-.401 -- fluctuating but consistently effective without querying
